title: The Context Window Constraint
concept: In-context learning requires feeding examples in the prompt, consuming valuable tokens and increasing the cost and latency of every generation.

options.text: The limit of text an AI can read and write in one go
The physical size of the computer screen
The speed of the user's internet connection

exercises.explain: The context window defines the boundary of what the model can 'see' and process during a single chat or request.

cards.title: The Prompt Space Limit
cards.body: We know that giving an AI examples in its prompt (In-Context Learning) makes it super smart. But did you know there is a physical limit to how much it can remember at once, and it gets slower and pricier the more you feed it? Let's explore the context window constraint!

cards.title: What is the Context Window Constraint?
cards.body: The context window is the maximum number of words or tokens an AI can read and write at one time. In-context learning requires putting your training examples right inside the prompt. This takes up valuable space, leaving less room for the actual conversation!

cards.title: The Desk Analogy
cards.body: Think of your LLM like a desk space. In-context learning is like putting reference books and files directly on your desk to help you write. It works great, but the more papers you stack up, the less room you have to write new things, and it takes you much longer to scan through them all!

cards.title: The Repeating Cost in Action
cards.body: Imagine you want an AI to classify customer emails. Instead of training it, you paste 20 long examples of past emails directly into the prompt. The AI gets the job done, but you are now billed for sending those same 20 examples with every single new email you want classified.

cards.title: Myth: More Context is Always Better
cards.body: People think that since modern models have massive context windows, we can just stuff them with infinite examples. In reality, large prompts cost more money, take longer to process (latency), and sometimes cause the AI to 'forget' details in the middle!

cards.title: Context Window Allocation
cards.body: Comparing prompt structures with and without heavy in-context learning. Notice how examples eat up your available workspace!

left.text: Huge prompt with 30 examples + user question. Slow & expensive.

right.text: Tiny prompt with just the user question. Fast & cheap.

exercises.prompt: Why does in-context learning increase the cost of every single generation?

options.text: The AI permanently memorizes them, making future prompts shorter.
You send the examples with every prompt, increasing token usage and processing time.
The model becomes completely free to use.

exercises.explain: Because the AI must process all the historical examples you pasted inside the prompt alongside your new question every single time, the cost and latency increase.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In-context learning is constrained by the maximum limit of tokens an AI can process at once.
exercises.explain: AI models have a strict limit on the number of tokens they can process in a single request, known as the context window constraint.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete this rule of prompt design.
exercises.sentence: Adding more examples to your prompt consumes valuable context space by using up more tokens.
exercises.explain: Every word, letter, or code snippet we paste as an example consumes tokens, leaving less room for the AI's response.

exercises.prompt: Which system design is actively suffering from the context window constraint?

options.text: System A: A system that runs fast because it has zero examples.
System B: A system that sends 50 example conversations with every user query.
System C: A system that translates text without using a context window.

exercises.explain: System B represents standard in-context learning where you must pay the token cost of the examples on every single query, whereas System A uses fine-tuning to bake the knowledge in.

exercises.prompt: Why is relying heavily on in-context learning considered a 'costly' design pattern for production systems?

options.text: It permanently updates the AI's internal brain weights for free.
It consumes valuable prompt space and increases cost and latency for every run.
It automatically shrinks the prompt to make generations faster.
It completely eliminates the need for any input tokens.

exercises.explain: In-context learning forces you to send examples with every single query, meaning you pay for those same example tokens over and over again, while also slowing down response times due to the large prompt size.
